items.body:
Founded in 2013 under International Wine & Beverage Company, CheckMate focuses on ultra premium Chardonnay and Merlot, showcasing the potential of the Okanagan with a strong sense of place. The estate leans into a refined, modern luxury style while staying true to its terroir, with a cohesive chess theme that reflects the precision of the wines.

On the Merlot side, the South Okanagan, particularly Osoyoos, provides the heat needed to build structure and depth, resulting in wines that feel more powerful than many Ontario expressions. The Chardonnays were the standout, especially the 2021 Little Pawn, which showed vibrant citrus, layered fruit, and impressive texture while remaining fresh and balanced.

Overall, the experience is elevated from start to finish, with thoughtful winemaking, a stunning property, and strong hospitality. It comes at a premium, but you are paying for more than just the wine, it is the full experience.
items.title: CheckMate Artisanal Winery

items.body:
Founded in 1993, Burrowing Owl Estate Winery is a well-established South Okanagan producer with a beautiful property and a warm, welcoming feel. The setting stands out, relaxed, scenic, and easy to enjoy.

That said, the wines didn’t fully deliver for me. Everything was refined, but I found them lacking depth and intensity compared to what the region can offer. The tasting room experience was also a bit flat and less engaging than others in the valley.

I have seen Burrowing Owl quite a bit in Ontario as well, which isn’t super common for Okanagan wineries and speaks to how established they are.

Where they really win is the food, easily the highlight. Great quality, well done, and paired with very reasonable pricing. Overall, while the wine and service didn’t stand out, the property, food, and value still make it worth a visit.
items.title: Burrowing Owl Estate Winery

items.body:
Set just outside Okanagan Falls, Liquidity is one of those places where the setting really sticks with you. It’s a smaller property, but in the best way, and with Skaha Lake right there, it’s easily one of the best views in the South Okanagan in my opinion.

The wines lean refined and balanced. The whites are zippy, fresh, and perfect for summer, and honestly I liked everything across the lineup. Nothing feels forced, it all just drinks well.

Service was excellent. It’s the kind of place you go, have a few glasses, take in the view, and end up staying longer than you planned. I’d go out of my way to visit this one again.
items.title: Liquidity Winery

items.body:
Quails’ Gate has been around for a long time, with the Stewart family farming the land for over a century before opening the winery in 1989, and it’s now part of IWBC. It’s known for Pinot Noir and Chardonnay and is one of the more established names in the Okanagan.

For me though, nothing really stood out. The property felt a bit mid overall nice, but not somewhere I’d go out of my way for. That said, the restaurant has a strong reputation and is probably the main draw. The one thing I did really enjoy was the port, which was easily the highlight for me.
items.title: Quails Gate

items.body:
Two Sisters Vineyards in Niagara-on-the-Lake is a newer spot that’s built a strong name pretty quickly. The property is honestly unreal the architecture and high ceilings are beautiful, and everything feels very polished and well put together.

The whites were solid, nothing crazy but definitely enjoyable. Where it really stood out for me was the overall experience. The restaurant was phenomenal, and the rabbit gnocchi was insane easily one of the best dishes I’ve had at a winery.

Prices were pretty solid too for the level they’re operating at. Even if the wines didn’t fully stand out to me, the property and restaurant alone make it worth going out of your way for.
items.title: Two Sisters Vineyards
